# Pre-lecture Videos: Friend or Foe

## Description

This website acts as documentation for the University of 
  North Carolina at Chapel Hill's Computer Science 110 course.
  These data display slight correlation between students who displayed
  interest in pre-lecture videos and students who viewed the course as
  moderately difficult. Using such information, conclusions are formed 
  that show adding pre-lecture videos to COMP 110 could provide students 
  with the supplementary information they need to feel successful in the course. 

  After analyzing the results of the data from Alyssa Lytle's course survey, many students found the course monderatly to extremely difficult. After this finidng we wondered what could be donne to improve student learning? One possible solution we analyzed was providing pre-lecture videos for students. 

## Data and Analysis

This found that there was some correlation between students who found comp 110 difficult and who belive there shoudl be pre-lecture videos in the class. Those who thought the course was a lower difficulty showed less indication of wanting additional videos. 


<img src="static\imgs\chart1.png" width="300">





Further data display that most students found the course moderatly difficult. The bell shape indicates averages of level 4-5 difficulty, which, in turn, could show a need for additional help for students outside of traditional class hours. 


<img src="static\imgs\chart2.png" width="300">


Our final graph shows the true correlation between our data. What we found is that students who ranked the class as being more difficult, also expressed greater interest in pre-lecture videos. Considering that many students find the course to be moderately difficult, releasing pre-lecutre videos would likely benefit a large portion of COMP-110 students.




<img src="static\imgs\chart3.png" width="400">


# Conclusions 
The graphs created using the "pre-lecture-videos" and "difficulty" show that a majority of the data concentrated between a level 4 and 5 difficulty. This shows that there is some correlation between students who believe there should be pre-lecture videos and students who believe the course is relativly difficult. Due to this correlation we reccomend that the class provide short pre-lecture videos that can indruce the general topics of the class before the more detailed in-person lecture. These videos could include lists of vocabulary for the day so students can match coding language with tasks they must execute once they are in class.

While pre-lecture videos could provide students with greater clarity, the potential costs could include some students no longer attending class due to the perception that videos before class cover the full content of that day's course. I think that this would be a necessary trade-off as the students who require additional practice and information would use the videos in the right way. The stakeholders of this new idea would be the TAs and the teachers as they would be the ones needing to put in extra hours to provide such videos. This could be negative to the Tas and or teachers as due to their intense an already packed schedules and may be unable to put in the extra time to produce the videos. Additional stakeholders would be the students as they would make the ultimate decision as to whetehr or not they should utilize the resource of a pre-lecture video. 

Overall the graph shows that there could be a positive impact of filming and allowing students to watch pre-lecture videos if the is an ability to film and ditribute the videos, and if the students themselves could take the initiative to watch the videos and put in the effort to understand the content before truly understanding the content in the class.
